9	# ADR-027: Skill Install Topology and Resolution Precedence
10	
11	**Status:** Accepted
12	**Date:** 2026-06-01
13	
14	## Context
15	
16	DDx ships the `ddx` skill as a package, and operators need to know which
17	install layer they are actually using. Before this decision, the install
18	topology was spread across implementation details and a handful of docs:
19	
20	- the project-local package install under `<project>/.ddx/plugins/ddx/`;
21	- the global fallback under `${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`;
22	- the baked-in default package embedded in the binary;
23	- the agent-facing skill outputs under `.agents/skills/` and
24	  `.claude/skills/`.
25	
26	Without one documented precedence rule, `ddx doctor` could not tell whether a
27	project was using its own package copy or falling through to the global layer,
28	and the agent docs had no single place to describe where the skill surfaces
29	live.
30	
31	## Decision
32	
33	DDx resolves the default `ddx` package in this order:
34	
35	1. project-local `<project>/.ddx/plugins/ddx/`
36	2. global `${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`
37	3. baked-in package embedded in the binary
38	
39	The project-local package is the authoritative install for a repository. The
40	global layer is a fallback and may be used when the project copy is absent.
41	The baked-in layer exists only for the default `ddx` package so the binary
42	remains usable offline.
43	
44	The agent-facing skill outputs are project-local and live in:
45	
46	- `<project>/.agents/skills/ddx/`
47	- `<project>/.claude/skills/ddx/`
48	
49	Home-directory skill installs are retired. DDx does not add new state under
50	`~/.agents/skills` or `~/.claude/skills`.
51	
52	## Consequences
53	
54	- `ddx doctor` can report the project install and the global fallback as
55	  distinct states.
56	- Operators can distinguish `ok`, `missing`, and
57	  `lazy-resolves-to-global` for the project layer.
58	- The docs can point agents and operators to the same project-local surfaces
59	  instead of describing competing home-directory and project-directory
60	  locations.
61	- The default package remains available offline through the embedded layer.
62	
63

2	name: ddx
3	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "what's blocking the queue", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
4	---
5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
11	forwards raw passthrough constraints for harness, provider, model,
12	and profile while power travels as `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds;
13	Fizeau owns concrete routing, provider/model discovery, aliases,
14	fuzzy matching, catalog lookups, and transcript/session rendering), a
15	persona system (bindable AI personalities), a library registry
16	(plugins with prompts, templates, personas), and git-aware
17	synchronization. This skill makes any skills-compatible coding agent
18	(Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, etc.) understand and operate
19	the DDx surface correctly.
20	
21	## How this skill works
22	
23	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
24	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
25	
26	Install locations: this skill is resolved from the project-local plugin layer
27	(`.ddx/plugins/ddx/`), the global machine layer
28	(`${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`), or the binary's baked-in
29	default — see **Install topology** below. `ddx doctor` reports which layer is
30	active.
31	
32	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
33	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
34	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
35	guidance, not this overview alone.**
36	
37	## Install topology
38	
39	DDx resolves the default `ddx` package in three layers: project-local
40	`.ddx/plugins/ddx/`, then the global fallback at
41	`${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`, then the baked-in package
42	embedded in the binary. `ddx doctor` reports the project and global
43	layers separately so operators can tell whether the project copy is
44	real, missing, or lazily resolving to the global layer.
45	
46	The agent-facing skill outputs live in the project-local
47	`.agents/skills/ddx/` and `.claude/skills/ddx/` surfaces. Home-directory
48	skill installs are retired; do not rely on `~/.agents/skills` or
49	`~/.claude/skills`.
50	
51	## Vocabulary
52	
53	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
54	exact definitions.
55	
56	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
57	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
58	- **Queue** — the set of active beads. The *execution-ready queue* is
59	  `status=open` work whose dependencies are closed and which is not parked by
60	  cooldown, supersession, or execution-eligibility policy. `ddx bead ready`,
61	  `ddx bead status`.
62	- **Lifecycle status** — the persisted bead state: `proposed`, `open`,
63	  `in_progress`, `blocked`, `closed`, or `cancelled`.
64	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
65	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
66	- **Blocked** — `status=blocked` work paused by a hard external recheckable
67	  blocker. Ordinary dependency waits are derived queue state, not blocked
68	  status. `ddx bead blocked`.
69	- **Proposed** — work awaiting operator decision before autonomous execution.
70	  Proposed beads are not worker-eligible until accepted, rewritten, split,
71	  blocked externally, cancelled, or otherwise resolved.
72	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
73	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
74	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
75	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
76	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
77	- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
78	  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
79	  raw passthrough constraints.
80	- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
81	  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
82	  finalization.
83	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
84	  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
85	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
86	  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
87	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
88	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
89	  below).
90	- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
91	  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
92	  branch on harness names.
93	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
94	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
95	  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
96	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
97	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
98	  personas.
99	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
100	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
101	- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
102	  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
103	  Fizeau owns concrete route selection within those bounds.
104	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
105	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
106	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
107	  `ddx install <name>`.
108	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
109	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
110	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
111	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
112	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
113	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
114	  orchestration; that remains harness business.
115	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
116	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
117	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
118	  `ddx upgrade`.
119	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
120	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
121	  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
122	  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
123	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
124	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
125	  via `spec-id`.
126	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
127	  at its governing artifact.
128	
129	## Intent router
130	
131	Before responding, read the matching file.
132	
133	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
134	|---|---|
135	| "what should I work on next", "what's blocking the queue", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", broad queue orientation or planning | `reference/interactive.md` |
136	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
137	| `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "start the worker", "start N workers and watch", "monitor the drain", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
138	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
139	| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
140	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
141	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
142	
143	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
144	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
145	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
146	
147	## Top-level policy reminders
148	
149	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
150	reference file; do not violate them.

1	# Task Dispatch — Power, Persona, Passthrough
2	
3	> **Debugging routing?** Routing lives entirely in Fizeau. See `CONTRACT-003-fizeau-service` (in the agent repo, `docs/helix/02-design/contracts/`) for the public surface; routing internals are inside that module.
4	
5	DDx invokes task execution through the upstream Fizeau service. DDx does not
6	choose harnesses, providers, endpoints, or models on the normal work path. It
7	describes the work, forwards raw passthrough constraints for harness,
8	provider, model, and profile unchanged, requests an abstract power
9	level, and lets Fizeau route. Fizeau owns concrete routing,
10	provider/model discovery, alias resolution, fuzzy matching of raw model
11	strings, transcript/session rendering, and all route-side errors.
12	
13	## Power bounds
14	
15	Normal execution should use power bounds:
16	
17	```bash
18	ddx run --min-power 10 --prompt task.md
19	```
20	
21	Power is an integer scale exposed by the Fizeau contract. DDx passes
22	`MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` bounds and treats them as ordering
23	constraints, not as model identities. DDx does not query a model catalog,
24	discover providers, or pin concrete routes on the normal work path. Fizeau
25	reports what it actually used:
26	
27	```text
28	running with qwen 3.6-27b (power 10)
29	```
30	
31	DDx owns bead retry policy. It decides whether an attempt succeeded using DDx
32	evidence: commits, no-changes rationale, merge/preserve result, gates, review
33	verdicts, and cooldowns. If a retry is allowed, DDx may raise `MinPower`.
34	
35	Fizeau owns route selection within those requested bounds. DDx may raise
36	`MinPower` on retries, but it must not pin a concrete model/provider on the
37	normal work path or infer route selection from a catalog.
38	
39	## Passthrough constraints
40	
41	`--harness`, `--provider`, `--model`, and `--profile` are
42	allowed only as passthrough constraints:
43	
44	```bash
45	ddx run --min-power 10 --model qwen36 --prompt task.md
46	```
47	
48	DDx sends these values to Fizeau unchanged. DDx does not validate them,
49	score them, use them for queue policy, rewrite them on retry, or use them as a
50	fallback mechanism. Fizeau owns any fuzzy matching, alias resolution, provider
51	fallback, or typed error for those raw strings. DDx stops on hard-pin
52	exhaustion; it does not remove pins, widen pins, call route-selection helpers,
53	or retry in a loop.
54	
55	## Execution layering
56	
57	The intended command layering is:
58	
59	- `ddx run` — one Fizeau `Execute` call with requested `MinPower`/`MaxPower`.
60	  No route-selection helper.
61	- `ddx try <bead>` — one bead attempt layered over `run`.
62	- `ddx work` — queue drain and retry policy layered over `try`.
63	
64	Route diagnostics are status/debug-only. Do not use diagnostic route decisions
65	in `run`, `try`, or `work`, and never feed one back into `Execute`.
66	
67	## Personas
68	
69	Personas are reusable AI personality templates. DDx injects a
70	persona's body as a system-prompt addendum to `ddx run` —
71	same persona, same behavior across every harness, because the
72	harness sees a flat system prompt, not a persona file.
73	
74	### Default roster
75	
76	The default `ddx` plugin ships five role-focused personas:
77	
78	- `code-reviewer` — security-first review with structured verdicts
79	- `test-engineer` — stubs-over-mocks, real-e2e, baselined
80	  performance, testing pyramid as shape not ratios
81	- `implementer` — YAGNI / KISS / DOWITYTD, ships tests with code
82	- `architect` — opinionated on when to reach for each pattern
83	  (monolith-first, data-model-first)
84	- `specification-enforcer` — refuses drift from governing artifacts
85	
86	See `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md` for the quality bar and
87	authoring guidance. Projects can install additional personas via
88	plugins.
89	
90	### Using a persona
91	
92	Directly at invocation time:
93	
94	```bash
95	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
96	```
97	
98	Or by binding a role in `.ddx/config.yaml`:
99	
100	```yaml
101	persona_bindings:
102	  code-reviewer: code-reviewer
103	  test-engineer: test-engineer
104	  architect: architect
105	```
106	
107	Workflows then reference the role abstractly (e.g., "dispatch to
108	the `code-reviewer` role"), and DDx resolves the binding at
109	dispatch time.
110	
111	```bash
112	ddx persona list                # available personas
113	ddx persona show <name>         # persona body + frontmatter
114	ddx persona bind <role> <name>  # set a binding
115	ddx persona bindings            # current role → persona map
116	```
117	
118	## Quorum
119	
120	For multi-agent review or adversarial checks, use a workflow skill composition
121	such as `compare-prompts`, covered in `reference/review.md`:
122	
123	```bash
124	ddx run --persona code-reviewer --prompt review.md
125	ddx run --persona architect --prompt review.md
126	```
127	
128	## Getting more capabilities
129	
130	`ddx install <name>` adds plugins to the project. Plugins can ship
131	personas, prompts, patterns, templates, and workflow skills.
132	
133	```bash
134	ddx install helix              # HELIX workflow plugin
135	ddx search <term>              # discover available plugins
136	ddx installed                  # list installed plugins
137	ddx uninstall <name>           # remove
138	```
139	
140	Custom personas go in `.ddx/plugins/<plugin>/personas/<name>.md`
141	(or directly in `library/personas/<name>.md` for local-only use).
142	See the personas README for the authoring quality bar.
143

146	DDx resolves package installs in this order: project-local

148	`${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/<name>/`, then the baked-in

12	// GlobalPluginDir returns the filesystem path for a named plugin in the global
13	// tier: ${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/<name>.
14	// The directory may not exist; callers that need it present must create it.
15	func GlobalPluginDir(name string) string {
16		return filepath.Join(ddxroot.GlobalDir(), "plugins", name)
17	}
18	
19	// ResolvePlugin returns the on-disk path and the resolution layer for a plugin.
20	// Precedence: project → global → baked-in.
21	//
22	// Layer values:
23	//   - "project": found under <projectRoot>/.ddx/plugins/<name>
24	//   - "global":  found under ${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/<name>
25	//   - "baked-in": embedded default; only valid for the "ddx" plugin name
26	//
27	// For baked-in, path is empty — callers should use defaultplugin.FS().
28	// Any other unresolved name returns a non-nil error.
29	func ResolvePlugin(ctx context.Context, projectRoot, name string) (path string, layer string, err error) {
30		projectPluginPath := filepath.Join(ddxroot.Path(ctx, projectRoot), "plugins", name)
31		if info, statErr := os.Stat(projectPluginPath); statErr == nil && info.IsDir() {
32			return projectPluginPath, "project", nil
33		}
34	
35		globalPluginPath := filepath.Join(ddxroot.GlobalDir(), "plugins", name)
36		if info, statErr := os.Stat(globalPluginPath); statErr == nil && info.IsDir() {
37			return globalPluginPath, "global", nil
38		}
39	
40		if name == "ddx" {
41			return "", "baked-in", nil
42		}
43	
44		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("plugin %q not found in project, global, or baked-in layers", name)
45	}

16	// commitTestFiles stages and commits the listed project-relative paths.
17	func commitTestFiles(t *testing.T, dir string, relPaths ...string) {
18		t.Helper()
19		cleanEnv := gitpkg.CleanEnv()
20		for _, rel := range relPaths {
21			add := exec.Command("git", "add", rel)
22			add.Dir = dir
23			add.Env = cleanEnv
24			require.NoError(t, add.Run(), "git add %s", rel)
25		}
26		commit := exec.Command("git", "commit", "--allow-empty", "-m", "test: commit update target files")
27		commit.Dir = dir
28		commit.Env = cleanEnv
29		require.NoError(t, commit.Run(), "git commit")
30	}

32	// writeRegistryInstalled writes a fake installed.yaml under ~HOME/.ddx/ so that
33	// performUpdate sees the listed files as registry-installed update targets.
34	func writeRegistryInstalled(t *testing.T, entries []registry.InstalledEntry) {
35		t.Helper()
36		state := &registry.InstalledState{Installed: entries}
37		require.NoError(t, registry.SaveState(state))
38	}

40	// TestUpdate_ForceReplacesStaleSymlinks verifies that ddx update --force
41	// replaces legacy symlinks under .agents/skills/ with real files.
42	// This covers the FEAT-015 requirement that skills.Install is always invoked
43	// with Force:true during update (via refreshShippedSkills).
44	func TestUpdate_ForceReplacesStaleSymlinks(t *testing.T) {
45		homeDir := t.TempDir()
46		t.Setenv("HOME", homeDir)
47	
48		te := NewTestEnvironment(t, WithGitInit(false))
49	
50		// Create .agents/skills/ddx as a symlink (pre-migration state).
51		agentSkillsDir := filepath.Join(te.Dir, ".agents", "skills")
52		require.NoError(t, os.MkdirAll(agentSkillsDir, 0o755))
53	
54		fakeTarget := filepath.Join(t.TempDir(), "old-global-ddx")
55		require.NoError(t, os.MkdirAll(fakeTarget, 0o755))
56		symlinkPath := filepath.Join(agentSkillsDir, "ddx")
57		require.NoError(t, os.Symlink(fakeTarget, symlinkPath))
58	
59		// Verify the symlink exists before update.
60		info, err := os.Lstat(symlinkPath)
61		require.NoError(t, err)
62		require.True(t, info.Mode()&os.ModeSymlink != 0, "expected symlink before update")
63	
64		// Run ddx update --force; this should call refreshShippedSkills which
65		// calls skills.Install with Force:true, replacing the symlink.
66		_, err = te.RunCommand("update", "--force")
67		// The command may fail due to network operations (checking plugin updates),
68		// but refreshShippedSkills runs unconditionally before any network call.
69		// We tolerate network errors but require the symlink to be gone.
70		_ = err
71	
72		// After update, the symlink must be replaced with a real directory.
73		info, err = os.Lstat(symlinkPath)
74		if os.IsNotExist(err) {
75			// Skill was not installed (embedded FS may not have ddx skill in test);
76			// acceptable — the symlink is gone either way.
77			return
78		}
79		require.NoError(t, err)
80		if info.Mode()&os.ModeSymlink != 0 {
81			t.Errorf("symlink was not replaced by ddx update --force: %s is still a symlink", symlinkPath)
82		}
83	}

149	
150		// File is clean — no modification after commit.
151	
152		output, err := te.RunCommand("update", "--force")
153	
154		// A network error from InstallPackage is tolerated; the test only asserts
155		// that the dirty-file guard did NOT fire.
156		_ = err
157		assert.NotContains(t, output, "--force --discard-local",
158			"clean file should not trigger the dirty-file error; got: %q", output)
159	}

161	// TestUpdate_DiscardLocalOverwritesDirty asserts that ddx update --force
162	// --discard-local succeeds when an installed file is dirty, and that the
163	// original content is saved to .ddx/update-backup/<timestamp>/<path> [AC3].
164	func TestUpdate_DiscardLocalOverwritesDirty(t *testing.T) {
165		homeDir := t.TempDir()
166		t.Setenv("HOME", homeDir)
167	
168		te := NewTestEnvironment(t) // git-initialised by default
169	
170		installedFile := filepath.Join("library", "skills", "ddx", "SKILL.md")
171		writeRegistryInstalled(t, []registry.InstalledEntry{{
172			Name:    "ddx",
173			Version: "0.4.7",
174			Type:    registry.PackageTypePlugin,
175			Files:   []string{installedFile},
176		}})
177	
178		te.CreateFile(installedFile, "original committed content\n")
179		commitTestFiles(t, te.Dir, installedFile)
180	
181		// Make dirty — this is the content that must be saved in the backup.
182		dirtyContent := "modified content — not yet committed\n"
183		te.CreateFile(installedFile, dirtyContent)
184	
185		// Run with --discard-local; tolerate network errors from InstallPackage.
186		output, err := te.RunCommand("update", "--force", "--discard-local")
187		_ = err
188	
189		// Must NOT produce the dirty-file error.
190		assert.NotContains(t, output, "--force --discard-local", /*as error msg*/
191			"--discard-local should suppress the dirty-file block; got: %q", output)
192	
193		// Backup directory must exist under .ddx/update-backup/<timestamp>/
194		backupRoot := filepath.Join(te.Dir, ddxroot.DirName, "update-backup")
195		entries, readErr := os.ReadDir(backupRoot)
196		require.NoError(t, readErr, "update-backup directory should be created")
197		require.NotEmpty(t, entries, "at least one timestamped backup dir should exist")
198	
199		// The backup file must contain the dirty (pre-overwrite) content so the
200		// operator can recover their uncommitted changes.
201		backupFile := filepath.Join(backupRoot, entries[0].Name(), installedFile)
202		data, readErr := os.ReadFile(backupFile)
203		require.NoError(t, readErr, "backup file should be readable: %s", backupFile)
204		assert.Equal(t, dirtyContent, string(data),
205			"backup should contain the dirty (pre-overwrite) content")
206	}

208	// TestUpdate_PartialDirtyBatchHaltsCleanly asserts that when multiple
209	// registry-installed files are slated for overwrite and even ONE is dirty, the
210	// entire update halts before any file is mutated — clean files remain untouched
211	// [AC4].
212	func TestUpdate_PartialDirtyBatchHaltsCleanly(t *testing.T) {
213		homeDir := t.TempDir()
214		t.Setenv("HOME", homeDir)
215	
216		te := NewTestEnvironment(t) // git-initialised by default
217

219		cleanFile := filepath.Join("library", "skills", "ddx", "reference", "work.md")
220		writeRegistryInstalled(t, []registry.InstalledEntry{{
221			Name:    "ddx",
222			Version: "0.4.7",
223			Type:    registry.PackageTypePlugin,
224			Files:   []string{dirtyFile, cleanFile},
225		}})
226	
227		te.CreateFile(dirtyFile, "dirty file content\n")
228		te.CreateFile(cleanFile, "clean file content\n")
229		commitTestFiles(t, te.Dir, dirtyFile, cleanFile)
230	
231		// Only modify the dirty file
232		te.CreateFile(dirtyFile, "modified and uncommitted\n")
233	
234		_, err := te.RunCommand("update", "--force")
235		require.Error(t, err, "update should fail when any target file is dirty")
236	
237		// The clean file must NOT have been mutated by the aborted update.
238		data, readErr := os.ReadFile(filepath.Join(te.Dir, cleanFile))
239		require.NoError(t, readErr)
240		assert.Equal(t, "clean file content\n", string(data),
241			"clean file must be unchanged after atomic refuse")
242	
243		// refreshShippedSkills must NOT have run — .agents/skills/ddx/ should
244		// not have been created.
245		_, statErr := os.Stat(filepath.Join(te.Dir, ".agents", "skills", "ddx"))
246		assert.True(t, os.IsNotExist(statErr),
247			".agents/skills/ddx should not exist after atomic refuse")

8	---
9	# ADR-027: Skill Install Topology and Resolution Precedence
10	
11	**Status:** Accepted
12	**Date:** 2026-06-01
13	
14	## Context
15	
16	DDx ships the `ddx` skill as a package, and operators need to know which
17	install layer they are actually using. Before this decision, the install
18	topology was spread across implementation details and a handful of docs:
19	
20	- the project-local package install under `<project>/.ddx/plugins/ddx/`;
21	- the global fallback under `${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`;
22	- the baked-in default package embedded in the binary;
23	- the agent-facing skill outputs under `.agents/skills/` and
24	  `.claude/skills/`.
25	
26	Without one documented precedence rule, `ddx doctor` could not tell whether a
27	project was using its own package copy or falling through to the global layer,
28	and the agent docs had no single place to describe where the skill surfaces
29	live.
30	
31	## Decision
32	
33	DDx resolves the default `ddx` package in this order:
34	
35	1. project-local `<project>/.ddx/plugins/ddx/`
36	2. global `${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`
37	3. baked-in package embedded in the binary
38	
39	The project-local package is the authoritative install for a repository. The
40	global layer is a fallback and may be used when the project copy is absent.
41	The baked-in layer exists only for the default `ddx` package so the binary
42	remains usable offline.
43	
44	The agent-facing skill outputs are project-local and live in:
45	
46	- `<project>/.agents/skills/ddx/`
47	- `<project>/.claude/skills/ddx/`
48	
49	Home-directory skill installs are retired. DDx does not add new state under
50	`~/.agents/skills` or `~/.claude/skills`.
51	
52	## Consequences
53	
54	- `ddx doctor` can report the project install and the global fallback as
55	  distinct states.
56	- Operators can distinguish `ok`, `missing`, and
57	  `lazy-resolves-to-global` for the project layer.
58	- The docs can point agents and operators to the same project-local surfaces
59	  instead of describing competing home-directory and project-directory
60	  locations.
61	- The default package remains available offline through the embedded layer.
62	
63

1	---
2	name: ddx
3	description: Operates the DDx toolkit for document-driven development. Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says "do work", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "execute a bead", "review this", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", "check against spec", "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "what's blocking the queue", "create a bead", "file this as work", "run an agent", "dispatch", "use a persona", "how am I doing", "ddx doctor", or mentions any ddx CLI command.
4	---
5	
6	# DDx
7	
8	DDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for
9	document-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work
10	items with acceptance criteria), a task-execution boundary (DDx
11	forwards raw passthrough constraints for harness, provider, model,
12	and profile while power travels as `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds;
13	Fizeau owns concrete routing, provider/model discovery, aliases,
14	fuzzy matching, catalog lookups, and transcript/session rendering), a
15	persona system (bindable AI personalities), a library registry
16	(plugins with prompts, templates, personas), and git-aware
17	synchronization. This skill makes any skills-compatible coding agent
18	(Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, etc.) understand and operate
19	the DDx surface correctly.
20	
21	## How this skill works
22	
23	The skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent
24	router**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.
25	
26	Install locations: this skill is resolved from the project-local plugin layer
27	(`.ddx/plugins/ddx/`), the global machine layer
28	(`${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`), or the binary's baked-in
29	default — see **Install topology** below. `ddx doctor` reports which layer is
30	active.
31	
32	**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the
33	matching reference file from the router table below. The router is
34	not optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's
35	guidance, not this overview alone.**
36	
37	## Install topology
38	
39	DDx resolves the default `ddx` package in three layers: project-local
40	`.ddx/plugins/ddx/`, then the global fallback at
41	`${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/ddx/`, then the baked-in package
42	embedded in the binary. `ddx doctor` reports the project and global
43	layers separately so operators can tell whether the project copy is
44	real, missing, or lazily resolving to the global layer.
45	
46	The agent-facing skill outputs live in the project-local
47	`.agents/skills/ddx/` and `.claude/skills/ddx/` surfaces. Home-directory
48	skill installs are retired; do not rely on `~/.agents/skills` or
49	`~/.claude/skills`.
50	
51	## Vocabulary
52	
53	Single source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these
54	exact definitions.
55	
56	- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with
57	  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.
58	- **Queue** — the set of active beads. The *execution-ready queue* is
59	  `status=open` work whose dependencies are closed and which is not parked by
60	  cooldown, supersession, or execution-eligibility policy. `ddx bead ready`,
61	  `ddx bead status`.
62	- **Lifecycle status** — the persisted bead state: `proposed`, `open`,
63	  `in_progress`, `blocked`, `closed`, or `cancelled`.
64	- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is
65	  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.
66	- **Blocked** — `status=blocked` work paused by a hard external recheckable
67	  blocker. Ordinary dependency waits are derived queue state, not blocked
68	  status. `ddx bead blocked`.
69	- **Proposed** — work awaiting operator decision before autonomous execution.
70	  Proposed beads are not worker-eligible until accepted, rewritten, split,
71	  blocked externally, cancelled, or otherwise resolved.
72	- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write
73	  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.
74	- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit
75	  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes
76	  `success` or `already_satisfied`.
77	- **Run** — one task invocation atom. `ddx run` calls Fizeau `Execute`
78	  once with prompt/config, `MinPower`/`MaxPower` bounds, and optional
79	  raw passthrough constraints.
80	- **Try** — one bead attempt in an isolated worktree. `ddx try <id>` wraps
81	  `ddx run` with bead prompt resolution, evidence capture, and merge/preserve
82	  finalization.
83	- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` picks ready beads and invokes
84	  `ddx try`; it owns queue iteration and retry policy.
85	- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010). Includes execution
86	  definitions, execution records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/runs/<id>/`.
87	- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)
88	  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see
89	  below).
90	- **Harness** — a Fizeau routing concept. DDx may pass `--harness` as
91	  an operator-supplied constraint, but DDx does not validate, rank, fallback, or
92	  branch on harness names.
93	- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that
94	  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt
95	  addendum to `ddx run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.
96	- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,
97	  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to
98	  personas.
99	- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in
100	  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.
101	- **Power bounds** — `MinPower` and optional `MaxPower` integers passed to the
102	  upstream execution service. DDx may raise `MinPower` on eligible retries;
103	  Fizeau owns concrete route selection within those bounds.
104	- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to
105	  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,
106	  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.
107	  `ddx install <name>`.
108	- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +
109	  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is
110	  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.
111	- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an
112	  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:
113	  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx does not specify subagent
114	  orchestration; that remains harness business.
115	- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.
116	  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.
117	- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.
118	  `ddx upgrade`.
119	- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**
120	  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its
121	  acceptance criteria. **Comparison/adversarial review** is a workflow skill
122	  composition over `ddx run`, not a core quorum flag. See `reference/review.md`.
123	- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's
124	  work: a FEAT-\*, SD-\*, TD-\*, or ADR-\* under `docs/`. Referenced
125	  via `spec-id`.
126	- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing
127	  at its governing artifact.
128	
129	## Intent router
130	
131	Before responding, read the matching file.
132	
133	| User says / asks about | Read this file |
134	|---|---|
135	| "what should I work on next", "what's blocking the queue", "review with fresh eyes", "fold in this guidance", "review again", "break this down into specs and beads", "make this testable", broad queue orientation or planning | `reference/interactive.md` |
136	| write/plan work, "create a bead", "file this as work", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |
137	| `ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", "drain the queue", "run the next bead", "start the worker", "start N workers and watch", "monitor the drain", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |
138	| "review this", "check against spec", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |
139	| "assess bead readiness", "score a bead", "triage a failed attempt", "refine a bead", bead authoring lint | `bead-lifecycle/` |
140	| "run an agent", "dispatch", harness/provider/model passthrough, power, effort, "use a persona", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |
141	| "what's on the queue", "what's ready", "how am I doing", health check, "ddx doctor", sync status | `reference/status.md` |
142	
143	If the intent spans multiple files (e.g., "create a bead and then
144	run it"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the
145	user which concept they mean rather than guessing.
146	
147	## Top-level policy reminders
148	
149	These apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every
150	reference file; do not violate them.
151	
152	- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go
153	  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt
154	  bead history and cannot be audited.
155	- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,
156	  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting
157	  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or
158	  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.
159	- **Preserve bead-attempt commit history.** Branches containing
160	  `ddx try` / `ddx work` execution commits carry an audit trail. **Never
161	  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only
162	  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.
163	  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these
164	  branches.
165	- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c
166	  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent
167	  its own isolated checkout. `ddx try` does this automatically;
168	  manual parallel work should too.
169	- **Power-first execution dispatch.** Default to `ddx run`/`ddx try`/`ddx work`
170	  with power bounds. `--harness`, `--provider`, and `--model` are passthrough
171	  constraints only; DDx must not use them for routing policy.
172	
173	## Links out
174	
175	- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.
176	- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your
177	  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,
178	  executions, and skills features.
179	- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at
180	  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.
181	- Open standard this skill conforms to:
182	  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).

12	// GlobalPluginDir returns the filesystem path for a named plugin in the global
13	// tier: ${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/<name>.
14	// The directory may not exist; callers that need it present must create it.
15	func GlobalPluginDir(name string) string {
16		return filepath.Join(ddxroot.GlobalDir(), "plugins", name)
17	}
18	
19	// ResolvePlugin returns the on-disk path and the resolution layer for a plugin.
20	// Precedence: project → global → baked-in.
21	//
22	// Layer values:
23	//   - "project": found under <projectRoot>/.ddx/plugins/<name>
24	//   - "global":  found under ${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/<name>
25	//   - "baked-in": embedded default; only valid for the "ddx" plugin name
26	//
27	// For baked-in, path is empty — callers should use defaultplugin.FS().
28	// Any other unresolved name returns a non-nil error.
29	func ResolvePlugin(ctx context.Context, projectRoot, name string) (path string, layer string, err error) {
30		projectPluginPath := filepath.Join(ddxroot.Path(ctx, projectRoot), "plugins", name)
31		if info, statErr := os.Stat(projectPluginPath); statErr == nil && info.IsDir() {
32			return projectPluginPath, "project", nil
33		}
34	
35		globalPluginPath := filepath.Join(ddxroot.GlobalDir(), "plugins", name)
36		if info, statErr := os.Stat(globalPluginPath); statErr == nil && info.IsDir() {
37			return globalPluginPath, "global", nil
38		}
39	
40		if name == "ddx" {
41			return "", "baked-in", nil
42		}
43	
44		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("plugin %q not found in project, global, or baked-in layers", name)
45	}

31	func InstallPackageFromRemote(pkg *Package, projectRoot string) (InstalledEntry, error) {
32		entry := InstalledEntry{
33			Name:        pkg.Name,
34			Version:     pkg.Version,
35			Type:        pkg.Type,
36			Source:      pkg.Source,
37			InstalledAt: time.Now(),
38		}
39	
40		// FEAT-015 early validation: if the package argument already declares a
41		// home-rooted Root.Target, reject it before doing any I/O. The post-
42		// manifest check below covers manifest-supplied targets after download.
43		if pkg.Install.Root != nil && strings.HasPrefix(pkg.Install.Root.Target, "~") {
44			return entry, fmt.Errorf("FEAT-015: Root.Target must be project-relative; got %s in package %s; update the manifest to use a relative path", pkg.Install.Root.Target, pkg.Name)
45		}
46	
47		tmpDir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "ddx-install-"+pkg.Name+"-*")
48		if err != nil {
49			return entry, fmt.Errorf("creating temp dir: %w", err)
50		}
51		defer func() { _ = os.RemoveAll(tmpDir) }()
52	
53		tarballURL := githubTarballURL(pkg.Source, pkg.Version)
54		extractedDir, err := downloadAndExtract(tarballURL, tmpDir)
55		if err != nil {
56			return entry, fmt.Errorf("downloading %s: %w", tarballURL, err)
57		}
58	
59		return installFromExtractedDir(pkg, extractedDir, projectRoot, entry)

data.output:
## Persona System

DDX includes a persona system that provides consistent AI personalities for different roles:

- **Personas**: Reusable AI personality templates (e.g., `code-reviewer`, `test-engineer`, `architect`)
- **Roles**: Abstract functions that personas fulfill (e.g., `code-reviewer`, `test-engineer`)
- **Bindings**: Project-specific mappings between roles and personas in `.ddx.yml`

Personas enable consistent, high-quality AI interactions across team members and projects. Projects bind specific personas to roles. See `library/personas/` for available personas and `library/personas/README.md` for detailed documentation.

Plugin and persona lookup follows a three-layer precedence: project-local (`<project>/.ddx/plugins/<name>/`) → global (`${XDG_DATA_HOME}/ddx/global/plugins/<name>/`) → baked-in binary default (only for the `ddx` plugin). The project layer always wins when present. See `docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-027-skill-install-topology.md` for the full decision record.

## When filing beads

Bead descriptions + AC must be sufficient context for a sub-agent to execute the work without operator hand-curation (principle P7, `docs/helix/06-iterate/reliability-principles.md`). Before invoking `ddx bead create`, walk the 8-criterion rubric in `docs/helix/06-iterate/bead-authoring-template.md`: title clarity, root cause with file:line, numbered AC with specific test names, wired-in assertion, `cd cli && go test` + lefthook gate, labels with cross-refs, parent + deps, standalone description (no `/tmp/*`, no chat references). Beads scoring < 7/8 will fail in autonomous drain.
